retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid functions as a supporter of the synthesis and maintenance of coenzyme A, a cofactor and acyl group carrier for other enzymes, and an acyl protein carrier in the fatty acid synthase complex. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin E (Tocopherol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin E is best known to function as a chain-breaking antioxidant that neutralizes the lipid peroxyl radicals during lipid peroxidation to prevent cyclic propagation of lipid peroxidation. This process is key in protecting the polyunsaturated fatty acids within the phospholipids of plasma membranes and plasma lipoproteins. [53] Vitamin K (P hylloquinone; Menaquinone)

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Discovery and Isolation
retrieved_docs.content: The molecule's chemical structure was determined by Williams in 1940. Elvehjem et al and Jukes et al demonstrated that pantothenic acid was a growth and "anti-dermatitis" factor for chickens. In the 1950s, one of the functional forms of pantothenic acid, coenzyme A (CoA), was discovered as the cofactor essential for the acetylation of sulfonamides and choline. In the mid-1960s, pantothenic acid was identified as a component of an acyl carrier protein (ACP) in the fatty acid synthesis complex. Fritz Albert Lipmann won the Nobel Prize in Physiology and Medicine "for his discovery of coenzyme A and its importance for intermediary metabolism." [2] All genomes sequenced to date encode enzymes that use coenzyme A as a substrate, the biosynthesis of which requires cysteine, pantothenate (vitamin B5), and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Pathophysiology -- Causes of Phenotypic Variation
retrieved_docs.content: MCADD is considered a "conformational disorder;" however, clinical manifestations of the disease span a broad spectrum of severity, even for those patients with the same genotype. The pathogenic phenotype of the disease appears dependent on additional extrinsic and intrinsic factors that are not clearly understood. [4] One extrinsic factor to consider is riboflavin. As a flavoenzyme, MCADD depends on adequate riboflavin derivatives for its function. A clinical trial testing MCAD activity in the lymphocytes of patients with MCADD reported increased enzyme activity after riboflavin supplementation. However, the genetic profile of the test individuals was not reported in this study. [16] A second study examined lipid and protein oxidative damage in patients with MCADD who were supplemented with carnitine or carnitine and riboflavin. They were compared to individuals who did not receive any supplementation and healthy controls. The study reported increased protein oxidative damage and altered antioxidant defense in unsupplemented and carnitine-supplemented patients. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B5 is exclusively a precursor in coenzyme A (CoA) synthesis and thus only directly affects this metabolic pathway. CoA has a role in hundreds of human biochemical reactions, such as cell growth, intermediary metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis. [14] [15] The structure of CoA functions as a carbonyl-activating group and as an acyl group carrier to help facilitate these various reactions. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Fundamentals -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid, or vitamin B5, is an essential water-soluble vitamin that acts as a key component of coenzyme A and phosphopantetheine, which are crucial to fatty acid metabolism. Pantothenic acid is widespread in foods. The AI (adequate intake) of pantothenic acid for adults is 5 mg/day. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Vitamin A (Retinol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is a generic descriptor for compounds that exhibit the biological activity of retinol and provitamin A carotenoids. Retinol is an unsaturated 20-carbon cyclic alcohol. Provitamin A carotenoids exhibit a 40-carbon basal structure with cyclic end groups and a conjugated system of double bonds. [51] [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B3 -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Niacin may be used in lipid control for secondary prevention as monotherapy in patients intolerant to statins. [16] According to the AACE/ACE, niacin reduces lipoprotein(a) levels, but the clinical significance of this effect remains uncertain. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin B1 (Thiamin)
retrieved_docs.content: Riboflavin functions as a component of the metabolically essential coenzymes FMN and FAD. FMN and FAD can serve as intermediates in electron transfer in redox reactions. As coenzymes, FAD and FMN are often bound to enzymes that are oxidases and dehydrogenases. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B3 -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action of niacin in dyslipidemia involves inhibition of lipolysis within adipose tissue, reduction in liver triacylglycerol formation, inhibition of synthesis of apolipoprotein B-100 and hepatic very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), impaired cholesterol biosynthesis, and lowering of the fractional catabolic rate of HDL-apolipoprotein A-1 (Apo A-1). Apo A-1, an activator of lecithin cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT), has a significant role in reverse cholesterol transport. [12] In addition to its reductive effects, niacin also increases lipoprotein lipase activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin B3 (Niacin)
retrieved_docs.content: Niacin functions as a component of the metabolically essential coenzymes NAD and NADP. These coenzymes act as hydride ion acceptors or donors in biological redox reactions. They also serve as coenzymes for dehydrogenases. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamins are vital micronutrients that cannot be synthesized endogenously or in insufficient amounts, and the principal means by which we get vitamins is through our diet. Vitamins can be classified as water-soluble or fat-soluble. The fat-soluble vitamins include vitamins A, D, E, and K. Fat-soluble vitamins play integral roles in a multitude of physiological processes such as vision, bone health, immune function, and coagulation. This review discusses the biochemistry, transport, and roles of these vitamins, highlighting deficiency syndromes and potential toxicities.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Micronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Water-Soluble Vitamins
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B2 (riboflavin): Vitamin B2 is crucial for redox reactions, where riboflavin is used as an electron carrier in the form of flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) and flavin mononucleotide (FMN). [7] [22] Additionally, riboflavin has an important role as an antioxidant, as it aids in the regeneration of glutathione. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin A -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Micronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Fat-Soluble Vitamins
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A (retinol): Vitamin A can be obtained as preformed vitamin A (all-trans-retinol and its esters) or provitamin A (β-carotene). Retinol is essential for vision, cell differentiation, and growth. [8] [9] Dietary vitamin A can be found in animal products, including liver, kidney, oils, dairy products, and eggs, and in the form of provitamin A in plant sources, such as leafy vegetables and yellow or orange fruits and vegetables. [8] The recommended dietary allowance (RDA) of vitamin A for healthy adults is 700 micrograms (mcg) per day (d) for women and 900 mcg/d for men. The RDA for children is 300 to 900 mcg/d, while in pregnant women, the RDA is 770 mcg/d and 1300 mcg/d in lactating women. [10]

Functionally, vitamin A is associated with antioxidant activity, as shown by the link “antioxidancia related_to vitamin A”. Together, these relationships illustrate that vitamin A functions both as a key active component in diverse multivitamin and supplement formulations and, in some contexts, as an ancillary (inactive) ingredient, while its chemical classification and enzymatic mechanisms underpin its antioxidant and physiological effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: The community organizes “vitaminy” as a central classification that unites individual vitamins (α‑tocopherol/α‑tocoferol, cholecalciferol (vitamin D₃), calcitriol, vitamin K, vitamin K₂, vitamin K₃, vitamin C/ascorbic acid, vitamin A, vitamin B₁, vitamin B₆, vitamin B₁₂, folic acid, β‑carotene, thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxocobalamin, hydroxokobalamin, retinoids, cholin) and their complexes, precursors, and analogues (e.g., dihydrotachysterol, retinoidy). These entities are hierarchically linked to broader groups such as fat‑soluble vitamins (parent of vitamin K and retinoids), steroid hormones (including the active vitamin D metabolite calcitriol and cholecalciferol), and the “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors” category. Multivitamin agents, multivitamin preparations, and multivitamin + mineral preparations are positioned within the vitaminy framework, exemplified by the prenatal multivitamin product (VITAMIN A acetate

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 125
retrieved_docs.content: or recommending supplements containing this compound. Collectively, vitamin A, niacin and calcium pantothenate are extensively integrated into complex supplement and pharmaceutical matrices, where they act synergistically to support cellular metabolism, antioxidant defense, vision, immune competence, energy production, lipid homeostasis and vascular health. Their dosage, formulation context and patient‑specific contraindications are critical considerations for safe and effective clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: formulations containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide) are classified as vitaminy, illustrating that both single‑nutrient supplements and compound formulations belong to the same hierarchy. Inverse‑ISA statements integrate combined entities such as “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two” and “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂” into the vitaminy class. Minerals are closely linked to vitamins; multivitamins that contain minerals are considered vitamins and are associated with the mineral group, which is a subset of selenium—a trace element that connects to broader heavy‑metal categories. Nutritional deficiency (نقص التغذية) is associated with both vitamins and minerals, emphasizing the clinical importance of adequate intake.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 46
retrieved_docs.content: Multivitamin preparations are organized under the broad “vitaminy” category, which also includes vitamin IV solution additives, multivitamin + mineral preparations, prenatal multivitamin formulations (containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide), and other complex vitamin‑mineral combinations. Individual vitamins are linked to “vitaminy” through hierarchical “isa”, “has parent”, “has child”, and “inverse_isa” relations: α‑tocopherol (vitamin E), vitamin K (including K₂ and K₃) as a parent for fat‑soluble vitamins, retinoids and vitamin A acetate (forms of vitamin A and vitamin D), and ascorbic acid (vitamin C) with its combination products (e.g., ascorbic acid + calcium). B‑vitamin groups are represented by the vitamin B complex, riboflavin, vitamin B₁ (alone or combined

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines “vitaminy” as a comprehensive class that encompasses individual vitamins (vitamin A, vitamin B₁, vitamin B₆, vitamin B₁₂, vitamin C [ascorbic acid], vitamin D, vitamin E [α‑tocopherol], vitamin K and its fat‑soluble subclasses including vitamin K₂/₃), their chemical derivatives, combination formulations, and related pharmaceutical preparations. Analogues and metabolites such as hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin, retinoids, tocopherols, cholecalciferol, kalcitriol, and other derivatives are linked to their parent vitamins through “has parent” relationships, while compounds such as cholin are treated as child entities within the vitamin taxonomy. Multivitamin preparations, multivitamin agents, multivitamin IV‑solution additives, and complex multivitamin products (e.g., formulations containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: pathophysiology. Vitamin K, another fat‑soluble vitamin, is present in multivitamin‑with‑iron tablets, demonstrating that combination products can deliver both water‑soluble B‑complex vitamins (niacin, calcium pantothenate) and fat‑soluble vitamins (A, D, K). Retinoids are correctly classified as a subclass of the vitamin A family rather than vitamin K, and pharmacologic retinoid agents such as Palovarotene (an inverse retinoid) are mentioned alongside vitamin A preparations that are often co‑formulated with vitamin D. Collectively, these relationships depict a hierarchical organization of nutrients—vitamin A family, vitamin B‑complex/B‑vitamin family, and fat‑soluble vitamins (A, D, K)—and illustrate how multivitamin‑mineral supplements integrate multiple active and inactive components to achieve comprehensive nutrient coverage. Understanding each component’s active versus inactive status, biochemical function, therapeutic role (e.g., bone health, metabolic support) and safety

Overall, the compiled evidence demonstrates that niacin is routinely incorporated as a biologically active component in diverse supplement products, contributing to the nutritional support of metabolic pathways, lipid regulation, and overall health, while its classification within the B Vitamin Family and its association with pantothenic acid reinforce its integral place in the vitamin B complex.

Recognizing niacin’s role within the B‑vitamin family and distinguishing it from other B vitamins such as pantothenic acid is essential for accurate formulation labeling and for clinicians advising patients on supplement use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base treats vitamins as a central micronutrient class that encompasses fat‑soluble vitamin K (with subtypes vitamin K₁, vitamin K₂ and vitamin K₃) and a broad array of water‑soluble B‑complex vitamins (vitamin B₁/thiamin, vitamin B₂/riboflavin, vitamin B₆/pyridoxine hydrochloride, vitamin B₁₂/cyanocobalamin, niacinamide, folic acid) together with vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate and essential minerals (calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide, cupric oxide). These agents are combined in the prenatal‑plus multivitamin tablet (VITAMIN A ACETATE 2000 IU / BETA CAROTENE 2000 IU / ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 400 IU / α‑TOCOPHEROL ACETATE 22 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.84 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACINAMIDE 20 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 10 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12 µg / CALCIUM CARBONATE 200 mg / FERROUS FUMARATE 27 mg / ZINC OXIDE 25 mg / CUPRIC OXIDE 2 mg),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 589
retrieved_docs.content: “Enzyme Interactions” is identified as a mechanism of action of vitamin A, reflecting its role in regulating gene expression and enzymatic pathways involved in vision, immune function, and cellular differentiation. The term “antioxidancia” is related to vitamin A, underscoring its antioxidant properties that help protect cellular membranes from oxidative damage. Structurally, vitamin A is classified under the structural class “retinol acetate”, indicating that many of its esterified forms (e.g., vitamin A acetate) share this chemical backbone. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that vitamin A, in its various esterified (acetate, palmitate) and provitamin (beta‑carotene) forms, is a central component of diverse nutraceutical, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic products. Its inclusion spans active therapeutic roles, supportive nutritional supplementation, and ancillary formulation functions, while its biochemical actions involve enzyme interactions and antioxidant activity, all

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The community groups all vitamin‑related agents under the class “vitaminy,” linking them to nutritional deficiency and to a broader chemical domain. Fat‑soluble vitamins form a sub‑hierarchy that includes vitamin A (retinoids), vitamin D (cholecalciferol, kalcifediol = 25‑hydroxy‑vitamin D, and kalcitriol = 1,25‑dihydroxy‑vitamin D), vitamin E (α‑tocoferol and related tokoferols), and vitamin K, while water‑soluble vitamins comprise vitamin C (ascorbic acid) and the B‑complex (thiamine B₁, riboflavin B₂, niacinamide B₃, pyridoxine B₆, cyanocobalamin B₁₂, hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin, and folic acid) together with their combination products. Multivitamin preparations are child concepts of “vitaminy” and illustrate co‑administration of multiple vitamins and essential minerals; examples include plain multivitamin tablets, liquid multivitamins such as IRON/MULTIVITAMS LIQUID, and prenatal multivitamins containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 99
retrieved_docs.content: across pharmaceutical products. Collectively, the community knowledge emphasizes that vitamin A, niacin, and calcium pantothenate are integral components of oral multivitamin‑mineral regimens, often co‑formulated to address diverse micronutrient needs. They serve both active therapeutic functions—cellular support, enzymatic activity, antioxidant protection, phosphate‑chelating and metabolic regulation—and, in certain topical or protein products, inactive excipient roles, underscoring their importance in nutritional supplementation, metabolic health, and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: biotin, calcium pantothenate), vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol) often paired with calcium sources (e.g., calcium oyster shell) to support calcium metabolism and inhibit bone resorption, vitamin E (α‑tocopherol), and essential minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine, selenium, chromium, molybdenum, vanadium, potassium iodide). Many formulations also contain omega‑3 fatty acids and antioxidants such as lutein and α‑lipoic acid. Collectively, vitamin A, niacin and calcium pantothenate function both independently and synergistically across diverse pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products. Their combined actions provide antioxidant protection, metabolic and lipid regulation, enzymatic support, immune competence, red‑blood‑cell formation, bone mineralization and overall nutritional adequacy, underscoring their broad relevance to patient care, therapeutic formulation design and clinical outcomes.
